nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, weedy freshwater habitats — backwaters, marshes, canals, ditches, and slow-moving swampy areas across peninsular Florida

decorPreferences: dense planting with hardy species (java fern, java moss, fast growers)
visual breaks and line-of-sight blockers to reduce male aggression

diet.notes: The American Flagfish accepts a wide variety of foods including flakes, pellets, frozen foods such as Artemia, Daphnia, and bloodworm, and live invertebrates. A substantial plant-matter component is important; algae wafers, spirulina flakes, blanched vegetables such as zucchini, and gel foods are all suitable. The species is notably effective at consuming hair algae and black beard algae directly from tank surfaces.

avoidWith: dwarf shrimp (cherry shrimp, ghost shrimp) — tend to be preyed upon
slow or timid fish susceptible to fin-nipping

summary: The American Flagfish is a North American native killifish from Florida swamps, valued for its effectiveness at consuming filamentous algae, including hair algae. Males display vivid red, blue, and white markings. Best kept as a pair or small group, with males generally territorial and prone to fin-nipping toward fancy-finned tankmates.
careNotes:
The American Flagfish is a native Florida pupfish and one of the few freshwater fish genuinely effective at consuming filamentous algae, including hair algae and black beard algae — a useful addition to planted tanks with algae problems. It is coldwater-tolerant and can be kept in unheated aquariums in temperate climates, though it thrives across a broad 18–28°C range.

Males are conspicuously colored with red, blue, and white markings reminiscent of the US flag, especially vivid during breeding. They are territorial during spawning and can become aggressive toward conspecifics and fin-nipping toward long-finned tankmates. A 1M:2F ratio spread across a 75L+ tank with visual breaks generally moderates aggression adequately. Suitable tankmates are robust, short-finned species such as danios, corydoras, and livebearers.

This species is NOT recommended for tanks containing fancy guppies, bettas, angelfish, or other long-finned fish — fin-nipping is a documented risk, particularly when the fish are underprovided with territory or companions. Dwarf shrimp tend to be treated as food.

Breeding is relatively straightforward: males form temporary territories and guard eggs (paternal care); eggs hatch in 7–14 days depending on temperature. A tight-fitting lid is advisable as the species has been known to jump.
breedingNotes: The American Flagfish is a fractional spawner, depositing eggs continuously when water temperatures are warm rather than in a single clutch. Males establish and defend territories, providing paternal care by fanning and guarding the egg mass. Incubation is temperature-dependent, typically 7–14 days. Fry are free-swimming shortly after hatching and accept Artemia nauplii. Best bred in a species-only or pair-only setup, as males can be disruptive to other tank inhabitants during spawning.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (50–55 mm), temperature (18–30°C), pH (6.5–8.5), hardness (36–357 ppm / ~gH 2–20), tank footprint (60×30 cm base for pair), and breeding behavior. Taxonomy (Cyprinodontiformes / Cyprinodontidae) confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed max TL 6.5 cm, native range (peninsular Florida, St. Johns and Ochlocknee River drainages south), taxonomy, and wild temperature range (18–22°C). FishBase wild temp max is narrower than hobby consensus (30°C); temperature ceiling averaged across all three sources to 28°C.

fish.breedingNotes: Males guard and fan the egg mass and provide paternal care; fry become free-swimming shortly after hatching and accept Artemia nauplii.
